title: NAD+ and Sirtuins: The Real Science of Cellular Aging
description: David Sinclair, NMN, resveratrol, NR: we analyze without hype the real evidence on NAD+ and sirtuins in aging. What do human studies say, not just mouse studies.

segments.voice: In 2019, Harvard geneticist David Sinclair published his book Lifespan and the anti-aging world erupted. NAD+, sirtuins, NMN, resveratrol. The supplement industry made millions. But what does the science really say beyond the media hype? What has been proven in humans and what remains just a promise based on mouse studies? We analyze it without filters today.
segments.visual: aging longevity research laboratory NAD science molecules

segments.voice: NAD+ is a coenzyme present in all living cells, fundamental for energy metabolism. Its acronym stands for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide. It acts as an electron carrier in the mitochondria during ATP production. But it also has a crucial regulatory role: it is the indispensable substrate for sirtuins, a group of deacetylase proteins with central roles in longevity.
segments.visual: NAD+ coenzyme mitochondria energy production cellular biology

segments.voice: Sirtuins are a family of 7 proteins, SIRT1 to SIRT7, that regulate key aging processes: DNA repair, gene expression, inflammation, mitochondrial metabolism, and stress response. They are activated under caloric restriction and intense exercise conditions. The problem is that for them to function, they need NAD+, and NAD+ levels drop by 40 to 60 percent between the ages of 20 and 60.
segments.visual: sirtuins proteins DNA repair epigenetics aging cellular regulation

segments.voice: Why does NAD+ decline? Several factors. With age, the activity of the CD38 enzyme increases, which degrades NAD+. PARP proteins, which consume NAD+ massively in response to accumulated genetic damage, also increase. In parallel, de novo synthesis decreases. It's a double problem: more is consumed and less is produced.
segments.visual: NAD decline aging CD38 enzyme PARP DNA damage depletion

segments.voice: In mice, the results of elevating NAD+ are spectacular. MIT studies with NMN in old mice showed reversal of muscular, cardiovascular, and metabolic aging markers. Old mice ran like young mice. Median lifespan increased by up to 20 percent in some models. Sinclair published in Cell in 2013 how restoring NAD+ reversed vascular muscle deterioration.
segments.visual: mice aging reversal laboratory experiment longevity study

segments.voice: But humans are not mice. The big question is what happens in clinical trials with people. The most studied NAD+ precursors are NR, nicotinamide riboside, and NMN, nicotinamide mononucleotide. A 2018 trial in Nature Communications with 120 elderly adults showed that 500 milligrams of NR daily increased NAD+ levels in blood and muscle by 40 to 60 percent. That's clear. The problem is what happened next.
segments.visual: NMN NR supplements clinical trial blood NAD levels humans

segments.voice: Increasing NAD+ in blood has not yet translated into clear clinical benefits in humans. A 2024 meta-analysis in Ageing Research Reviews that gathered 23 clinical trials with NR and NMN concluded: yes, supplements consistently increase plasma NAD+. But the effects on fatigue, cognitive function, muscle strength, or inflammatory markers are inconsistent, modest, and not always statistically significant.
segments.visual: clinical trial results inconsistent evidence meta-analysis aging

segments.voice: The MIB-626 trial from 2022, funded by the US NIA, was the most robust to date: 250 milligrams of NMN twice a day in 100 elderly adults for 10 weeks. Result: 40 percent higher NAD+ in blood. But no differences in mobility, grip strength, body composition, or cognitive function compared to placebo. Researchers noted that plasma NAD+ may not reflect what happens inside cells.
segments.visual: NMN trial elderly supplements biomarkers no difference placebo

segments.voice: Sirtuins have the same problem: excellent results in animal models, scarce evidence in humans. Resveratrol, the most famous sirtuin activator found in red wine, worked wonders in mice. But the CALERIE trial at Harvard with 3 grams daily in humans showed no effects on longevity and biomarkers, and some participants developed severe gastrointestinal problems.
segments.visual: resveratrol red wine sirtuin activator human trial results

segments.voice: Bioavailability is the Achilles' heel of oral resveratrol. It is well absorbed but metabolized so quickly in the intestine and liver that its systemic bioavailability is less than 1 percent of the administered dose. Liposomal or piperine formulations improve absorption somewhat, but human data remains insufficient to recommend therapeutic doses.
segments.visual: bioavailability absorption metabolism supplement oral drug delivery

segments.voice: What does work with solid evidence to keep NAD+ high is exercise. A 2019 study in Cell Metabolism demonstrated that moderate resistance exercise increases muscle NAD+ by 20 to 30 percent in elderly adults, with associated improvements in mitochondrial function. Caloric restriction and intermittent fasting also elevate NAD+ by activating AMPK.
segments.visual: exercise NAD increase mitochondria aging muscle cell biology

segments.voice: Spermidine is another autophagy and longevity activator that doesn't require pills. Found in foods like wheat germ, aged cheese, mushrooms, and legumes, a 2018 prospective study in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition with 829 elderly individuals showed that high spermidine intake was associated with a 44 percent reduction in all-cause mortality over 20 years.
segments.visual: spermidine food sources wheat germ cheese mushrooms longevity diet

segments.voice: CD38 inhibitors are perhaps the most promising strategy to efficiently elevate NAD+. Apigenin, a flavonoid found in parsley, celery, and chamomile, inhibits CD38 and has been shown to increase muscle NAD+ in mice by up to 50 percent. There are no human trials yet, but the mechanism is biologically plausible and the compound is safe.
segments.visual: apigenin parsley celery quercetin flavonoid CD38 inhibitor

segments.voice: Where is the evidence now in 2025? NAD+ as a molecule is fundamental, that's not in doubt. That its levels drop with age is a fact. That elevating it in animals produces spectacular benefits, also. But translating that to humans without known side effects, with correct doses, and with clinically relevant endpoints remains a work in progress. NR and NMN supplements are probably safe, but paying 80 euros a month expecting proven results would be premature.
segments.visual: NAD supplements cost evidence human trial research progress

segments.voice: The biology of aging is one of the most exciting frontiers of science. NAD+, sirtuins, and longevity signaling pathways represent a genuine conceptual revolution. But rigorous science demands patience. Exercising, sleeping well, not smoking, eating real food, and managing stress elevate NAD+ and activate sirtuins in ways that no supplement has yet surpassed in controlled human trials. The basics still win.
segments.visual: healthy lifestyle exercise sleep diet aging prevention longevity
